author: marcopolo__
content:
I think we do a lot of what you're describing as part of https://github.com/libp2p/test-plans.

We have transport interop tests, hole punching tests, and gossipsub interop tests (with various gossipsub scenarios). The underlying tools you use depend on what exactly you're doing. Shadow works really well for interoping protocols like gossipsub for example.

author: marcopolo__
content:
Take a look at how the gossipsub interop works: https://github.com/libp2p/test-plans/tree/master/gossipsub-interop (and add dotnet-libp2p to that 🙂

New test cases can be implemented by writing a new script with the specified instructions. Implementations implement a simple interpreter for each instruction. The lets new tests be developed without needing any implementation changes.

author: __flcl
content: <@857052906495017000>  <@106441459183423488>  I want to be able to test such details in quic like handshake parameters, certificate details, packet reordering(so some of it can be counted as testing options agreed on by the certian network participants, others confirm protocol implementation is not buggy in general);  multiplexing limits and backpressure counting that may be also about yamux/qmux over tcp; pubkey exchange; more discovery packets testing; what happens if node cannot restore context from rlnc or gets gossip too lately. Share vulnerabilities fndings as tests. More like very isolated detail tests against (partial) reference implementation, but not limited to that. Current libp2p tests are more like interop + way less detailed that what I mean. Yeah I used those tests while writing .net libp2p, I'd say execution specs format would help me more.

author: __flcl
content: Thanks! Added an issue about gossipsub-interop. Btw benchmarking interop revealed so much more than transport-interop
